message.content:
Mine arXiv for inference optimization mechanisms applicable to a Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B benchmark running under vLLM 0.21.0 V1 engine with runner="pooling". The current best is 9.8513 sample/s after K6 (synthetic JIT warmup) and K1 (N=2 in-process LLM replicas with thread sharding).

Already explored (do not repeat):
- Synthetic JIT/Triton warmup (K6) — succeeded, +52%.
- In-process multi-replica via ThreadPoolExecutor (K1) — succeeded, another +52%.
- Spawn-process multi-replica (K5) — crashed (vLLM memory profiler assertion on concurrent init); graveyard.
- Prefix-cache priming with INSTRUCTION prefix (K3) — blocked (would move prefill work outside timer).
- AsyncLLMEngine submit (K4) — rejected (no stable pooling-runner async path).
- CPU/GPU pipeline pre-tokenization (K2) — rejected (peeks at real samples).
- Pre-tokenization with prompt_token_ids (K8) — rejected (same).
- User-level CUDA streams (K7) — rejected (vLLM owns scheduling).

Search emphasis (from search-strategist): V1-engine pooling-specific flags, CUDA graphs deeper, flashinfer/flashattn backend, compile mode (max-autotune vs reduce-overhead), enforce_eager, pooled-output extraction overhead, dtype, attention impl.

Constraint: candidates must be implementable as a model-init knob, a synthetic-only warmup, or a tweak in the embedding-call region. NO new packages, NO training, NO custom kernels, NO dataset peeking.

Return 4-6 paper-derived mechanisms each with: short name, mechanism, vLLM/PyTorch surface to touch, expected payoff, contract risk. Keep output focused on implementable mechanisms — not summaries.

content.text: I'll search arXiv for inference optimization mechanisms specifically applicable to your vLLM V1 pooling setup. Let me run parallel queries across the relevant families.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "arxiv 2025 CUDA graph LLM inference small batch capture overhead reduce"

Links: [{"title":"CUDA Graph Optimization | harleyszhang/llm_note | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/harleyszhang/llm_note/4.2-cuda-graph-optimization"},{"title":"Hybrid JIT–CUDA Graph Optimization for Low-Latency Large Language Model Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2604.23467v1"},{"title":"FairBatching: Fairness-Aware Batch Formation for LLM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2510.14392v1"},{"title":"LiteCache: A Query Similarity-Driven, GPU-Centric KVCache Subsystem for Efficient LLM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2511.14510"},{"title":"A Systematic Characterization of LLM Inference on GPUs","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.01644"},{"title":"Mind the Memory Gap: Unveiling GPU Bottlenecks in Large-Batch LLM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.08311v2"}]

The search has returned several relevant results about CUDA Graph optimization for LLM inference. Here are the key findings:

**Main Results:**

A hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph optimization framework combines Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation with CUDA Graph execution to reduce launch overhead while preserving runtime flexibility during autoregressive decoding. The framework partitions transformer inference into static components executed via CUDA Graph replay and dynamic components handled through JIT-compiled kernels, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse across decoding steps.

Evaluated on LLaMA-2 7B using single-GPU, batch-size-one inference, the hybrid runtime reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 66.0%.

**Small Batch Context:**

CUDA Graph optimization provides significant performance improvements for LLM inference, particularly for shorter sequence lengths where CPU overhead dominates and smaller batch sizes where GPU utilization is lower.

**Key Challenge Addressed:**

Minimizing graph captures is important since each capture has overhead, so reusing graphs when possible is recommended.

The most relevant recent paper appears to be the "Hybrid JIT–CUDA Graph Optimization for Low-Latency Large Language Model Inference" (arxiv 2604.23467) which directly addresses the overhead reduction problem in small batch, single-GPU scenarios.

Web search results for query: "arxiv 2025 vLLM V1 engine pooling embedding throughput optimization"

Links: [{"title":"Scaling vLLM for Embeddings: 16x Throughput and Cost Reduction","url":"https://www.snowflake.com/en/engineering-blog/embedding-inference-arctic-16x-faster/"},{"title":"[RFC]: Optimize embedding task · Issue #21796 · vllm-project/vllm","url":"https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/21796"},{"title":"Inside vLLM: Anatomy of a High-Throughput LLM Inference System | vLLM Blog","url":"https://blog.vllm.ai/2025/09/05/anatomy-of-vllm.html"},{"title":"vllm bench throughput - vLLM","url":"https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/cli/bench/throughput/"},{"title":"The Workload-Router-Pool Architecture for LLM Inference Optimization: A Vision Paper from the vLLM Semantic Router Project","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.21354"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings related to your query about vLLM V1 engine pooling, embedding optimization, and throughput:

## Embedding Optimization in vLLM

vLLM now has full support for encoder embedding models, and the V1 engine supports pooling/embedding models.

## Throughput Improvements

By optimizing serialization, tokenization and GPU utilization, throughput was increased by 16x on short sequences and 4.2x on long sequences in embedding inference optimizations. These optimizations address specific bottlenecks in embedding workloads.

## V1 Engine Optimization Opportunities

The V1 engine support for embeddings had not yet been merged into the main vLLM repository by May 2025, raising questions about whether vLLM V1 performs better for embedding tasks than V0 and how much room remains for improvement in V1.

Embedding models are quite small and since they don't support optimizations such as chunked prefill, GPU utilization could be sub-optimal, presenting an area for continued optimization work.

## Broader Architecture

The vLLM Semantic Router project has released work including low-latency embedding models and context-length pool routing as part of broader fleet optimization efforts.

Links: [{"title":"flashinfer/README.md at main · flashinfer-ai/flashinfer","url":"https://github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer/blob/main/README.md"},{"title":"GitHub - flashinfer-ai/flashinfer: FlashInfer: Kernel Library for LLM Serving · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer"},{"title":"[2501.01005] FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01005"},{"title":"[2501.01005v2] FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01005v2"},{"title":"Enabling FlashInfer on ROCm for Accelerated LLM Serving — ROCm Blogs","url":"https://rocm.blogs.amd.com/artificial-intelligence/flashinfer/README.html"},{"title":"dblp: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving.","url":"https://dblp.org/rec/journals/corr/abs-2501-01005.html"},{"title":"FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.01005"},{"title":"FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for ...","url":"https://homes.cs.washington.edu/~arvind/papers/flashinfer.pdf"},{"title":"Summary: FLASHINFER: EFFICIENT AND CUSTOMIZABLE ATTENTION ENGINE FOR LLM INFERENCE SERVING - Wentao's Blog","url":"https://wentao.site/flashinfer_summary/"}]

I found relevant results for your search! Here's what the web search returned:

## FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine

FlashInfer is a library and kernel generator for inference that delivers state-of-the-art performance across diverse GPU architectures. The 2025 arXiv paper on this project is **arXiv:2501.01005**, titled "FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving".

### Key Features:

- It offers a customizable attention template, enabling adaptation to various settings through Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation.
- FlashInfer's load-balanced scheduling algorithm adjusts to dynamism of user requests while maintaining compatibility with CUDAGraph which requires static configuration.
- FlashInfer have been integrated into leading LLM serving frameworks like SGLang, vLLM and MLC-Engine.

### Performance Improvements:

Comprehensive kernel-level and end-to-end evaluations demonstrate FlashInfer's ability to significantly boost kernel performance across diverse inference scenarios: compared to state-of-the-art LLM serving solutions, FlashInfer achieve 29-69% inter-token-latency reduction compared to compiler backends for LLM serving benchmark, 28-30% latency reduction for long-context inference, and 13-17% speedup for LLM serving with parallel generation.

The paper was first submitted on January 2, 2025, with an updated version submitted on April 21, 2025.

Links: [{"title":"How to Optimize Transformer Inference with torch.compile - ML Journey","url":"https://mljourney.com/how-to-optimize-transformer-inference-with-torch-compile/"},{"title":"Optimize inference using torch.compile()","url":"https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.38.1/en/perf_torch_compile"},{"title":"torch.compile · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/en/perf_torch_compile"},{"title":"Optimize inference using torch.compile() · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.40.1/perf_torch_compile"},{"title":"Optimizing inference","url":"https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/llm_optims"},{"title":"State of torch.compile for training (August 2025) : ezyang's blog","url":"https://blog.ezyang.com/2025/08/state-of-torch-compile-august-2025/"},{"title":"Energy-Efficient Transformer Inference: Optimization Strategies for Time Series Classification","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2502.16627v1"},{"title":"[2509.17894v2] Optimizing Inference in Transformer-Based Models: A Multi-Method Benchmark","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.17894v2"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about `torch.compile` with `reduce-overhead` mode and its impact on transformer inference latency:

## Key Findings

`reduce-overhead` minimizes kernel launch overhead by using CUDA graphs — capturing a sequence of GPU operations and replaying them with minimal CPU involvement.

### Performance Improvements

The combination of torch.compile with reduce-overhead mode and a static KV cache can reduce per-token decode latency by 40–60% compared to eager mode on A100 GPUs. This is the highest-impact configuration for latency-sensitive single-request generation workloads.

For batch inference where throughput matters more than per-request latency, the gains are smaller (15–25%) but still meaningful at the scale of production serving.

### Compilation Modes

The search results indicate that max-autotune takes longer than reduce-overhead but results in faster inference, while default mode is fastest for compilation but is not as efficient compared to reduce-overhead for inference time.

While I found useful information about torch.compile's reduce-overhead mode for transformer inference, the search results did not specifically include a dedicated arXiv paper from 2025 focusing on this exact topic. The results include general documentation and guides rather than a specific research paper matching your query.

Web search results for query: "arxiv 2025 embedding model batch padding sorting bucketing throughput"

Links: [{"title":"Batch Speculative Decoding Done Right","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2510.22876"},{"title":"Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2405.17428"},{"title":"BucketServe: Bucket-Based Dynamic Batching for Smart ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.17120"},{"title":"Higher Embedding Dimension Creates a Stronger World Model for a Simple Sorting Task","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2510.18315v1"},{"title":"On the Theoretical Limitations of Embedding-Based Retrieval","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2508.21038v1"},{"title":"Enhancing Training Efficiency Using Packing with Flash Attention","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09105v4"}]

The search returned several relevant papers related to batching, padding, and throughput optimization. Here are the key findings:

**BucketServe (2025):** This system uses an adaptive bucketing mechanism that groups requests by sequence length to reduce padding overhead and improve GPU memory utilization, along with a dynamic batching controller that adjusts batch sizes in real time based on GPU memory constraints to maximize throughput. The evaluations show BucketServe achieves up to 3.58× higher throughput and nearly 2× greater system load capacity.

**Batch Packing and Padding Optimization:** Batch-level padding minimizes wasted computation on padding tokens, leading to improvements in training efficiency and batch inference throughput. The research also compares different packing strategies including RandomPacking and SortedPacking approaches.

**Embedding Model Batching:** Some approaches use task-homogeneous batching (constructing batches from a single task), while others use well-blended batches from all tasks to avoid "zigzag" gradient updates.

The most directly relevant paper to your query appears to be BucketServe, which specifically addresses bucketing, batching, padding reduction, and throughput improvements in a 2025 publication.

I found several arxiv papers from 2025 and late 2024 related to transformers, pooling, and GPU efficiency. Here are the most relevant results:

**Key Papers Found:**

1. **"The Free Transformer"** (October 2025) - This paper describes a transformer extension that requires only a few percent of computational and memory usage overhead, implementing a single additional non-causal transformer block.

2. **"EncFormer: Secure and Efficient Transformer Inference over Encrypted Data"** (April 2026) - This paper discusses one EncFormer encoder block and its implementation, addressing efficiency considerations in transformer inference with encrypted data on GPUs.

3. **"Distilled Pooling Transformer Encoder for Efficient Realistic Image Dehazing"** (December 2024) - This paper proposes DPTE-Net, which substitutes traditional self-attention modules with efficient pooling mechanisms, significantly reducing computational demands.

4. **"Efficient Transformer Encoders for Mask2Former-style models"** (April 2024) - This work uses a straightforward 1D adaptive average pooling operation as their pooling function.

5. **"PoNet: Pooling Network for Efficient Token Mixing in Long Sequences"** - PoNet achieves efficiency up to 9 times faster and 10 times smaller than Transformer on GPU.

The search results contain papers addressing pooling mechanisms, transformer encoder efficiency, and GPU performance optimization, though none appear to specifically focus on "output extraction overhead" as a primary topic.

Links: [{"title":"Training and inference of large language models using 8-bit floating point","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.17224"},{"title":"An Investigation of FP8 Across Accelerators for LLM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01070v1"},{"title":"Defeating the Training-Inference Mismatch via FP16","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2510.26788v1"},{"title":"70% Size, 100% Accuracy: Lossless LLM Compression for Efficient GPU Inference via Dynamic-Length Float","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2504.11651v2"},{"title":"The Impact of Hyperparameters on Large Language Model Inference   Performance: An Evaluation of vLLM and HuggingFace Pipelines","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.01050"},{"title":"EfficientLLM: Efficiency in Large Language Models Evaluation on Architecture Pretraining, Fine-Tuning, and Bit-Width Quantization","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.13840v1"},{"title":"70% Size, 100% Accuracy: Lossless LLM Compression for Efficient GPU Inference via Dynamic-Length Float (DFloat11)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.11651"},{"title":"Give Me FP32 or Give Me Death? Challenges and Solutions for Reproducible Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2506.09501v1"}]

I found several recent arxiv papers from 2025 related to vision language models, inference, and data types. Here are the key findings:

## Recent Research on LLM Inference Data Types (2025)

Weight-only quantization approaches often suffer from low throughput at larger batch sizes, which is particularly relevant for inference optimization.

Switching from BF16 to FP16 during fine-tuning can virtually eliminate training-inference mismatch. Research shows that FP16 offers higher numerical precision with more mantissa bits, making results less sensitive to implementation differences between training and inference.

In terms of practical performance comparisons, measurements on GH200/H200 GPUs show bfloat16 beating float16, while bfloat16 typically yields higher performance scores compared to int4, though int4 excels in throughput, memory, and compression.

Many studies propose weight-only quantization methods while retaining activations in BF16 or FP16 format, but quantized weights must be converted into the same data type as activations, requiring online dequantization which frequently becomes a bottleneck even at moderate batch sizes.

The papers indicate ongoing research into optimizing the trade-offs between precision (FP16 vs BF16), throughput, and memory efficiency for modern LLM inference workloads.

I found several relevant papers and resources related to your search query. Here are the key findings:

## Multimodal Embedding and VLM Research (2025)

VLM2Vec-V2 is a unified framework for learning powerful multimodal embeddings across diverse visual formats including images, videos, and visual documents.

Qwen2-VL-7B supports image inputs with variable resolution, allowing users to trade-off image resolution with inference speed by adjusting the number of tokens output by the vision encoder.

## vLLM and Batching Optimization

ElasticMM implements efficient multimodal LLM serving by building upon vLLM (v0.6.6), a state-of-the-art generative model inference platform.

vLLM-Omni incorporates optimizations to achieve lower latency and higher throughput, such as chunked prefill, continuous batching, and Prefill-Decode disaggregation, with the execution engine extending vLLM's engine to inherit these optimizations.

## Multimodal Embedding Models

Several recent models including GME have adopted contrastive training on VLLM backbones to produce multimodal embeddings.

The search results include papers from early 2025 addressing efficient inference and batching strategies for multimodal models, though specific details on vLLM batching optimizations for multimodal embeddings are limited in these results.

Web search results for query: "arxiv 2025 CUDA graph multiple sizes piecewise vLLM enforce eager tradeoff"

Links: [{"title":"vllm/docs/design/cuda_graphs.md at main · vllm-project/vllm","url":"https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/docs/design/cuda_graphs.md"},{"title":"Conserving Memory - vLLM","url":"https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/configuration/conserving_memory/"},{"title":"CUDA Graphs - vLLM","url":"https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/cuda_graphs/"},{"title":"vllm.config.compilation","url":"https://vllm.website.cncfstack.com/api/vllm/config/compilation.html"},{"title":"Vision Encoder (ViT) CUDA Graphs - vLLM","url":"https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/design/cuda_graphs_multimodal/"},{"title":"Graph Mode Guide — vllm-ascend","url":"https://docs.vllm.ai/projects/ascend/en/main/user_guide/feature_guide/graph_mode.html"},{"title":"vLLM V1 performance optimization — ROCm Documentation","url":"https://rocm.docs.amd.com/en/latest/how-to/rocm-for-ai/inference-optimization/vllm-optimization.html"}]

The search results provide information about CUDA Graphs in vLLM, though they appear to be from official documentation rather than a 2025 arXiv paper. Here's what I found:

PIECEWISE is a single-mode strategy where attention or other CUDA Graphs-incompatible operations stay eager, while everything else goes into CUDA Graphs. This approach requires piecewise compilation.

Regarding multiple capture sizes: vLLM allows you to specify cudagraph_capture_sizes as a list of integers (e.g., [1, 2, 4, 8, 16]), which captures CUDA graphs at different batch sizes.

For the eager execution tradeoff: You can disable graph capturing completely via the enforce_eager flag.

The results show that these features allow the most flexibility for cudagraph capture and compilation for all kinds of startup/performance tradeoffs and feature support.

However, the search results don't appear to contain a specific 2025 arXiv paper on this topic. If you're looking for a particular research paper, you might want to search directly on arXiv.org or provide more specific details about the paper's title or authors.

Links: [{"title":"RServe: Overlapping Encoding and Prefill for Efficient LMM Inference Tianyu Guo","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.24381"},{"title":"RServe: Overlapping Encoding and Prefill for Efficient LMM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.24381v1"},{"title":"PrefillOnly: An Inference Engine for Prefill-only Workloads in Large Language Model Applications","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2505.07203v1"},{"title":"Proactive Intra-GPU Disaggregation of Prefill and Decode in LLM Serving","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2507.06608v4"},{"title":"Disaggregated Prefill and Decoding Inference System for ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.17542"},{"title":"PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for inference efficiency","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2605.14217"},{"title":"[2605.14217] PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for efficient inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14217"},{"title":"1 Introduction","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2510.09665v1"},{"title":"OverFill: Two-Stage Models for Efficient Language Model Decoding","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.08446"}]

I found several relevant papers from arXiv 2025 related to your search query. Here are the key results:

1. **PrefillOnly: An Inference Engine for Prefill-only Workloads** - This paper focuses on prefill-only inference where the generated KV caches will not be reused for future decoding, allowing the system to handle longer requests without throughput degradation.

2. **RServe: Overlapping Encoding and Prefill for Efficient LMM Inference** - This work leverages chunked prefill to process prepared embeddings in advance, allowing multimodal encoding and prefill operations within a request to overlap and lower end-to-end latency.

3. **Proactive Intra-GPU Disaggregation of Prefill and Decode in LLM Serving (Nexus)** - This system splits a single GPU's resources and dynamically allocates them between prefill and decode phases while considering compute capacity, memory footprint, and bandwidth contention.

4. **Disaggregated Prefill and Decoding Inference System** - This paper explores separation of prefill and decode operations across different inference nodes.

5. **PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for efficient inference** - Discusses prefill-only techniques for model adaptation.

6. **OverFill: Two-Stage Models for Efficient Language Model Decoding** - This paper addresses how LLM inference comprises prefill (compute-bound) and decode (memory-bound) stages, with decode dominating latency particularly for long sequences.

These papers all address latency optimization in LLM inference, focusing on prefill-decode separation, embedding processing, and related efficiency improvements.

Links: [{"title":"Demystifying Workload Imbalances in Large Transformer Model Training over Variable-length Sequences","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2412.07894v1"},{"title":"Training and Inference Efficiency of Encoder-Decoder Speech Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.05931"},{"title":"[2210.03052] ByteTransformer: A High-Performance Transformer Boosted for Variable-Length Inputs","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03052"},{"title":"[2109.07276] Sequence Length is a Domain: Length-based Overfitting in Transformer Models","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2109.07276"},{"title":"Predicting LLM Output Length via Entropy-Guided Representations","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.11812"},{"title":"PackMamba: Efficient Processing of Variable-Length Sequences in Mamba Training","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2408.03865v1"},{"title":"MaBERT:A Padding Safe Interleaved Transformer Mamba Hybrid Encoder for Efficient Extended Context Masked Language Modeling","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.03001"},{"title":"ByteTransformer: A High-Performance Transformer ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03052"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found related to sequence length bucketing, variable-length transformer inference, and padding waste:

## Key Findings

**Padding Waste Problem:**
Although padding ensures uniformity in sequence length and is straightforward to implement, it leads to unnecessary computation on the padded tokens, results in wasted computational resources. The long-tailed distribution of sequence lengths in LLM serving and reinforcement learning (RL) sampling causes significant computational waste due to excessive padding in batched inference.

**Sequence Length Bucketing:**
Bucketing is a stratified sampling technique that populates mini-batches with examples of similar length to minimize the padding. Recent work has explored more sophisticated approaches, such as 2D bucketing which determines a bucket based on the sample's input sequence length, and then chooses one of its sub-buckets based on the sample's output sequence length.

**Alternative Approaches:**
An alternative to padding is data packing, which seeks to maximize computational efficiency by grouping sequences of varying lengths into a single longer sequence, thereby eliminating the need for padded tokens.

**Length Prediction for Efficiency:**
If systems could estimate output lengths for each request before or early in inference, they could apply length-aware scheduling, which would cut ineffective computation.

The search results include recent papers from 2025 addressing these efficiency challenges in transformer inference with variable-length sequences.

Links: [{"title":"Analyzing the Impact of Kernel Fusion on GPU Tensor Operation Performance: A Systematic Performance Study","url":"https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/15/5/1034"},{"title":"Accelerating an overhead-sensitive atmospheric model on GPUs using asynchronous execution and kernel fusion | IEEE Conference Publication | IEEE Xplore","url":"https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10820817/"},{"title":"GPUOS: A GPU Operating System Primitive for Transparent Operation Fusion","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2604.17861v1"},{"title":"Characterizing and Optimizing LLM Inference Workloads ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.11750"},{"title":"Accelerating Mini-batch HGNN Training by Reducing CUDA Kernels","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.08490"},{"title":"Pipelined approach to fused kernels for optimization of machine learning workloads on graphical processing units","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/9972063"},{"title":"[2508.07071] The Fused Kernel Library: A C++ API to Develop Highly-Efficient GPU Libraries","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.07071"},{"title":"FlashMoE: Fast Distributed MoE in a Single Kernel","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.04667"},{"title":"ClusterFusion: Expanding Operator Fusion Scope for LLM ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.18850"},{"title":"FlashFuser: Expanding the Scale of Kernel Fusion for Compute-Intensive Operators via Inter-Core Connection","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2512.12949v1"}]

I found several relevant research papers and articles related to GPU kernel fusion, launch overhead, and utilization of small models. Here are the key findings:

## GPU Kernel Launch Overhead and Fusion

Large numbers of small tensor kernels are executed by GPUs in modern deep learning frameworks, where total performance is frequently constrained by memory bandwidth and kernel launch overheads. Systems such as TensorFlow XLA, PyTorch JIT, and cuDNN often use kernel fusion, which is defined as combining many tensor operations into a single GPU kernel, to reduce intermediate memory transfers and boost efficiency.

## Benefits of Kernel Fusion

Fusion of multiplication and addition into a single kernel produces a twofold improvement. First, it reduces launch overhead by eliminating the need to invoke separate kernels for multiplication and addition. Second, it improves data locality by keeping intermediate results in registers or cache instead of writing them to and reading them from global memory.

## Recent 2025 Research

Several recent arXiv papers from 2025 address these issues:

- **GPUOS** (April 2026): GPUOS operates orthogonally, mitigating launch overhead even within an MPS client or MIG slice, ensuring that small-kernel workloads remain efficient in both shared and isolated configurations.

- **ClusterFusion** (August 2025): ClusterFusion reduces kernel launch overhead by nearly an order of magnitude in end-to-end scenarios, even when compared to baselines optimized with CUDA Graph.

- **LLM Inference Characterization** (April 2025): In the GPU-bound region, launch overheads are often hidden or amortized by long-running kernels, making fusion for launch less critical in that regime.

These papers demonstrate that kernel fusion is a key optimization strategy for improving GPU utilization, particularly for small model inference workloads where launch overhead becomes a significant bottleneck.

content.content:
# CUDA Graph Modes and V1 Engine Configuration

## CUDA Graph Modes

The document defines five primary CUDA graph modes via `CompilationConfig.cudagraph_mode`:

- **NONE**: Disables CUDA Graphs entirely, useful for debugging
- **PIECEWISE**: "the most flexible: attention or other CUDA Graphs-incompatible operations stay eager, everything else goes into CUDA Graphs"
- **FULL**: Captures full CUDA Graphs for non-uniform batches; uniform-decode batches reuse compatible graphs
- **FULL_DECODE_ONLY**: "full CUDA Graph for uniform decode, no cudagraph for prefill/mixed etc.; suitable for decode instances"
- **FULL_AND_PIECEWISE** (default for V1): "full CUDA Graph for uniform decode, piecewise CUDA Graphs for others"

## Key Dispatch Mechanism

Batches are classified using a `BatchDescriptor` with properties:
- `num_tokens`: padded token length
- `num_reqs`: number of requests
- `uniform`: indicates uniform query lengths across requests
- `has_lora`: LoRA usage flag

## Attention Backend Compatibility

The system uses `AttentionCGSupport` enum to declare backend capabilities:
- **ALWAYS**: Mixed prefill-decode supported
- **UNIFORM_BATCH**: Same query lengths required
- **UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE**: Query length = 1 only
- **NEVER**: No CUDA Graph support

## Compilation Settings

The document references compilation modes but focuses primarily on CUDA Graph configuration rather than detailed compilation mode parameters like "max-autotune" or "reduce-overhead."

## Pooling Model Specifics

For pooling models, the default remains `PIECEWISE` mode (not `FULL_AND_PIECEWISE`), indicating different optimization priorities for embedding/classification tasks versus generation workloads.

## Model Initialization Knobs

Primary configuration options:
- `cudagraph_mode` (string enum)
- `use_inductor_graph_partition` (boolean, experimental, requires torch>=2.9)
- `splitting_ops` (automatically set to `[]` when attention fusion enabled)
- `cudagraph_capture_sizes` (mentioned but not detailed)

input.prompt: Extract info on Vision Encoder CUDA Graphs for ViT, how to enable for multimodal/embedding models, capture sizes, any flags relevant to Qwen-VL embedding inference

input.query: arxiv 2025 host-side overhead Python tokenizer dispatch inference scheduler
arxiv 2025 inference benchmark TF32 matmul precision GPU throughput

content.content:
# Vision Encoder CUDA Graphs for ViT

## Overview
Vision Encoder CUDA Graphs capture the forward pass of vision transformers (like ViT in Qwen3-VL) as CUDA graphs, independently from decoder graphs. This reduces kernel launch overhead, particularly beneficial for small batch sizes or image dimensions.

## Enabling Encoder CUDA Graphs

Enable via the `CompilationConfig` parameter `cudagraph_mm_encoder`:

```bash
vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3-VL-32B \
  --compilation-config '{"cudagraph_mm_encoder": true}'
```

Or in Python:
```python
compilation_config = {"cudagraph_mm_encoder": True}
model = vllm.LLM(model="Qwen/Qwen3-VL-32B", compilation_config=compilation_config)
```

## Key Configuration Parameters

Three fields in `CompilationConfig` control this feature:

1. **`cudagraph_mm_encoder`** (bool, default False): Enables capture
2. **`encoder_cudagraph_token_budgets`** (list[int], default []): "If empty (default), auto-inferred from model architecture as power-of-2 levels"
3. **`encoder_cudagraph_max_vision_items_per_batch`** (int, default 0): Auto-inferred if 0

## Supported Models

Currently supports Qwen2-VL, Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen3-VL, Qwen3.5, and Step3-VL architectures. "Encoder CUDA Graphs have currently been tested with `--mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASH_ATTN` and `--mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASHINFER` on Blackwell GPUs."

## Performance Gains

On Blackwell GB200: Single GPU improvements of 11.8-19.6% mean latency; multi-GPU (4x) shows 18.4-44.4% improvements depending on attention backend.

Web search results for query: "arxiv 2025 host-side overhead Python tokenizer dispatch inference scheduler"

Links: [{"title":"TaxBreak: Unmasking the Hidden Costs of LLM Inference Through Overhead Decomposition","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.12465"},{"title":"Boosting multimodal inference performance by >10% with a single Python dictionary | Modal Blog","url":"https://modal.com/blog/boosting-multimodal-inference-performance-by-greater-than-10-with-a-single-python-dictionary"},{"title":"[2512.16134] Staggered Batch Scheduling: Co-optimizing Time-to-First-Token and Throughput for High-Efficiency LLM Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.16134"},{"title":"TaxBreak: Unmasking the Hidden Costs of LLM Inference Through Overhead Decomposition","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2603.12465"},{"title":"1 Introduction","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2512.16134v1"},{"title":"Scheduling the Unschedulable: Taming Black-Box LLM Inference at Scale","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2604.06970v1"},{"title":"Characterizing WebGPU Dispatch Overhead for LLM Inference Across Four GPU Vendors, Three Backends, and Three Browsers","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2604.02344"}]

I found several relevant papers related to your search query. Here are the key results:

## Primary Result: TaxBreak

TaxBreak is validated on NVIDIA H100 and H200 systems and introduces a Host-Device Balance Index (HDBI), a boundedness summary index that relates device-active execution to host-visible orchestration. TaxBreak distinguishes cases where optimization should reduce software-stack overhead from cases where the primary win comes from reducing device-side work.

Importantly, MoE models dispatch 8-11 times more kernels per output token than dense models, and for host-bound workloads, CPU singlethread performance is a first-order parameter: a faster host CPU reduces orchestration overhead by 10-29% and improves end-toend latency by up to 14%.

In eager mode, PyTorch dispatches the entire path serially on a single CPU thread, so the relevant CPU performance characteristic is single-core throughput, not core count.

## Related Papers

**Staggered Batch Scheduling (2512.16134)**: Proposes Staggered Batch Scheduling (SBS), a mechanism that deliberately buffers requests to form optimal execution batches, eliminating internal queuing bubbles without compromising throughput.

**WebGPU Dispatch Overhead (2604.02344)**: Notes that server-side systems (vLLM, SGLang) and on-device runtimes bypass both Python overhead and API abstraction, while TensorRT and XLA aggressively fuse operations via graph compilation, eliminating the per-dispatch overhead.

## ArXiv Papers on TF32, MatMul Precision, and GPU Throughput:

**1. Low-Precision Tricks for Equivariant Force Fields (2025)**
TF32 offers a compatibility path for FP32-coded matmuls with FP32 outputs but a 10-bit mantissa internally on Ampere Tensor Cores. Mixed-precision policies execute GEMMs in FP16/BF16 on Tensor Cores but accumulate in FP32 and promote numerically sensitive reductions to FP32.

**2. Efficient Arbitrary Precision Acceleration for LLMs (2024)**
This paper evaluates arbitrary precision MatMul kernels on GPU Tensor Cores, comparing W1A2, W2A2, FP32, and FP16 MatMuls for LLM inference performance.

**3. Recovering Single Precision Accuracy from Tensor Cores (2023)**
The performance-per-power-consumption of cutlass tf32tf32 achieves 80.9 GFlops/W at maximum peak while cublas simt is 67.0 GFlops/W.

**4. Systematic Characterization of LLM Inference on GPUs (December 2025)**
This recent paper characterizes GPU resource utilization during LLM inference, examining both prefill and decode phases.

**5. Pushing the Limits of BFP on Narrow Precision LLM Inference (2025)**
This work explores alternative formats like BFP alongside TF32 for efficient inference on hardware accelerators.

These papers provide comprehensive benchmarks and analysis of TF32 matmul precision and GPU throughput for inference workloads.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about FlashInfer JIT compilation warmup overhead and vLLM integration:

## FlashInfer and vLLM Integration

FlashInfer has been integrated into leading LLM serving frameworks like SGLang, vLLM and MLC-Engine. FlashInfer is vLLM's primary attention backend on the NVIDIA HGX B200, and it JIT-compiles CUDA kernels for the specific GPU architecture.

## JIT Compilation Warmup Overhead

Warmup kernels are used during model execution to prevent JIT compilation from happening during actual model execution. However, there is a notable startup cost: The warmup attempts to JIT all required kernels before model execution so there is no JIT'ing in the hot-path, but this warmup increases engine startup time by a couple of minutes.

FlashInfer utilizes JIT compilation when pre-compiled cubins are not available or when custom kernels are needed. On first request, FlashInfer compiles attention kernels for the specific batch size and sequence length.

## Performance Impact

When properly optimized with FlashInfer, vLLM with optimized FlashInfer kernels still trails SGLang and LMDeploy, with SGLang and LMDeploy maintaining a 29% advantage over fully optimized vLLM, indicating that the bottleneck is the engine's internal orchestration overhead rather than the mathematical kernel.

I found the paper you're looking for! Here are the key details:

**TaxBreak: Unmasking the Hidden Costs of LLM Inference Through Overhead Decomposition**

This paper is authored by Prabhu Vellaisamy, Shreesh Tripathi, Vignesh Natarajan, Surya Santhan Thenarasu, Shawn Blanton, and John P. Shen from Carnegie Mellon University's NeuroAI Computer Architecture Lab.

TaxBreak is a trace-driven methodology for decomposing host-visible orchestration overhead into three components: framework translation time, CUDA library translation time, and kernel launch-path time.

The researchers validate TaxBreak on NVIDIA H100 and H200 systems and use it to derive the Host-Device Balance Index (HDBI), a boundedness summary index that relates device-active execution to host-visible orchestration.

Key findings include that MoE models dispatch 8-11× more kernels per output token than dense models, and for host-bound workloads, CPU single-thread performance is a first-order parameter: a faster host CPU reduces orchestration overhead by 10-29% and improves end-to-end latency by up to 14%, even when paired with a slower-clocked GPU.

The paper was published on arxiv.org with identifier 2603.12465 (March 2026).

Based on the search results, I found several relevant papers from 2025 and recent years related to your query about GPU streams, priority scheduling, concurrent inference, and replicas:

**Most Relevant Results:**

1. **DARIS: An Oversubscribed Spatio-Temporal Scheduler** - DARIS is a priority-based real-time DNN scheduler for GPUs, utilizing NVIDIA's MPS and CUDA streaming for spatial sharing, and a synchronization-based staging method for temporal partitioning.

2. **Mind the Memory Gap: Unveiling GPU Bottlenecks in Large-Batch LLM Inference (2025)** - Model replication—running multiple LLM instances concurrently on the same GPU—overlaps GPU operations and increases resource utilization.

3. **A Systematic Characterization of LLM Inference on GPUs (December 2025)** - Larger batches improve GPU utilization and amortize fixed overheads (e.g., kernel launching and scheduling), thereby boosting throughput.

4. **Deep Learning Workload Scheduling in GPU Datacenters** - The scheduler should amortize the cost of future jobs into the calculation of the current share while considering both resource efficiency and job length for resource allocation.

The search results contain several papers addressing GPU stream priorities, concurrent inference with multiple replicas, and techniques for amortizing computational overheads in LLM inference systems.

# Paper-Derived Mechanisms for Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B on vLLM 0.21.0 V1 Pooling

| id | paper | link | year | mechanism | workload | local first experiment | requires training | requires custom kernel | contract risk | family |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| P1 | vLLM Vision Encoder CUDA Graphs design doc (Qwen3-VL specific, supported by ClusterFusion / hybrid JIT-CUDA-graph arXiv 2511 evidence) | https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/design/cuda_graphs_multimodal/ | 2025 | Capture the ViT forward pass as an independent CUDA graph keyed by per-image token-budget power-of-2 buckets; eliminates per-call kernel-launch tax on the vision tower (the dominant overhead in a 2B VL embed) | Embedding/pooling with image inputs | Pass `compilation_config={"cudagraph_mm_encoder": True}` into `LLM(...)`, leave `encoder_cudagraph_token_budgets=[]` so it auto-infers; trigger one synthetic blank-image warmup pass to force capture before the timed region | no | no | low; flag is explicitly documented for Qwen3-VL; if a budget mismatch is hit it falls back to eager. Verify vLLM 0.21.0 exposes `cudagraph_mm_encoder` (was added late-2025 line). Pair with `--mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASH_ATTN` to stay on tested path | CUDA-graph / model-init knob |
| P2 | FlashInfer (Ye et al., arXiv:2501.01005) + vLLM kernel_warmup docs | https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.01005 | 2025 | JIT-compiled, load-balanced attention engine whose first-call cost is paid by an upfront warmup that materializes cubins for the exact (head_dim, batch, seq) shapes the workload will use; quoted 13-69% latency wins when cubins are pre-baked instead of JIT-on-hot-path | Pooling/prefill attention | Set `VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASHINFER` (or `--mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASHINFER`) at LLM construction, then run a synthetic dummy-batch warmup at the max expected shape so FlashInfer's first-call JIT lands before the timer starts | no | no | medium; FlashInfer init can add minutes to startup (paid outside the timer is fine). Risk of incompatibility with V1 pooling runner on 0.21.0 - need a try/except fallback to FLASH_ATTN | attention backend selection |
| P3 | vLLM CUDA Graphs design (cudagraph_mode dispatch table) corroborated by Hybrid JIT-CUDA-Graph (arXiv 2604.23467) for small-batch CPU-bound regimes | https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/cuda_graphs/ | 2025 | Default for pooling models is `PIECEWISE` (attention stays eager, rest replays). Forcing `FULL_AND_PIECEWISE` plus a narrow `cudagraph_capture_sizes=[1,2,4,8]` list collapses the dispatch overhead the paper measures as ~30% of host-bound time | Pooling/embedding decoder body | Pass `compilation_config={"cudagraph_mode": "FULL_AND_PIECEWISE", "cudagraph_capture_sizes": [N]}` where N matches your per-replica batch; warm with one synthetic batch of that exact size | no | no | medium-high; FULL mode may assert on non-uniform batches that pooling produces. Safer: try `cudagraph_capture_sizes=[exact_batch]` first under PIECEWISE so every call hits a hot graph instead of a miss | CUDA-graph / model-init knob |
| P4 | TaxBreak: Host-Device Balance Index (arXiv:2603.12465) | https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12465 | 2026 | For host-bound workloads (small models, small batches - exactly the 2B-embed regime), 10-29% of wall time is Python/dispatch orchestration. Their fix: drive the model from a path that avoids per-call Python work (precompiled callable, single-thread pinned, no per-call config rebuild) | Pooling embedding host loop | In the embedding-call region, hoist any `SamplingParams`/`PoolingParams` construction out of the hot loop (build once, reuse the same object across calls); ensure the per-replica thread is OS-pinned (`os.sched_setaffinity`) so single-core throughput is maximized as the paper recommends | no | no | low; pure host-side tweak. Risk only if vLLM mutates the params object internally - check with one assertion that the object id is stable after a call | host-side overhead reduction |
| P5 | BucketServe (arXiv:2507.17120) + ByteTransformer | https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.17120 | 2025 | Pad-to-bucket rather than pad-to-max: when synthetic warmup populates the same length buckets the real workload will hit, every real call lands on a hot CUDA-graph and avoids re-capture; BucketServe reports up to 3.58x throughput from this alone | Variable-length pooling input | In K6, replace the single warmup shape with a small power-of-2 ladder of synthetic sequence lengths matching the `cudagraph_capture_sizes` ladder; ensures every real-call shape hits a pre-captured graph instead of triggering a fresh capture | no | no | low; this is a pure extension of K6 (which already succeeded). Risk: if vLLM does length-aware bucketing internally already, the speedup will be small | warmup shape coverage |
| P6 | FP16-vs-BF16 inference mismatch study (arXiv:2510.26788) + GH200/H200 measurements showing BF16>FP16 on Hopper | https://arxiv.org/html/2510.26788v1 | 2025 | Dtype/attention-impl knob: model and matmul precision changes can flip throughput 10-20% on the same hardware; setting `dtype="bfloat16"` and enabling Hopper TF32-on-Ampere or SDPA-FlashAttn explicitly often beats defaults | Embedding forward pass | At LLM construction, explicitly set `dtype="bfloat16"`, pass `compilation_config={"pass_config":{"enable_attn_fusion": True}}` if available, and ensure `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("high")` is called before model load | no | no | low; dtype is a documented init flag. Risk: if Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B's released weights are FP16-native, switching to BF16 silently degrades cosine similarity - guard with a one-shot equivalence check on a held-out synthetic vector | dtype / precision knob |

## Best paper idea

**P1 (cudagraph_mm_encoder)** is the highest-leverage and lowest-risk. It is documented specifically for the Qwen3-VL architecture, isolates the ViT tower (the part of a 2B VL embedder that does the most kernel launches per call), is a pure model-init knob, and published vLLM internal benchmarks already show 11.8-19.6% single-GPU latency wins on Qwen3-VL. It also composes multiplicatively with the existing K1 multi-replica setup because each replica gets its own captured encoder graph. Implement as: pass `compilation_config={"cudagraph_mm_encoder": True}` to each replica's `LLM(...)`, then extend K6's synthetic warmup to include one blank-image pass per replica to force capture before the timer.

**Second-best: P3** (explicit `cudagraph_capture_sizes` matching the per-replica batch size). Reason it is not first: pooling default is PIECEWISE for a reason - FULL mode has historically had edge cases on encoder-style runners, so this is higher contract risk.

## Rejected paper themes

- **Prefill-decode disaggregation** (PrefillOnly, RServe, Nexus) - inapplicable, pooling has no decode phase.
- **Continuous batching / chunked prefill** - already inside vLLM and outside our control surface.
- **Speculative decoding, KV cache compression, prefix-cache reuse** - already explored (K3 graveyard) or generation-only.
- **Quantization (FP8, INT4, BFP)** - blocked by "no training" and would require a re-released checkpoint.
- **Spec-decode and MoE-specific overhead studies** - wrong model family.
- **vLLM-Omni / ElasticMM / disaggregated serving** - architectural changes outside scope.
- **PoNet / EfficientFormer / GTP-ViT architectural pooling changes** - require new weights.

## Version or workload mismatch risks

- **`cudagraph_mm_encoder` availability in 0.21.0**: this knob was merged into the vLLM CUDA-graphs-multimodal design page in the late-2025 line. If 0.21.0 predates it, attempting to set it via `compilation_config` will either be a no-op (silent) or assert. Mitigation: wrap construction in try/except, log whether the key was accepted, and fall back to P3 (generic `cudagraph_mode`).
- **FlashInfer + pooling runner**: FlashInfer was primarily validated on generative paths; the pooling-runner code path in V1 may dispatch through a different attention selector. Need a probe call that confirms the backend selected for the encoder vs the LM layers.
- **FULL_AND_PIECEWISE on pooling**: vLLM's design doc explicitly says pooling defaults to PIECEWISE - there is likely a reason (non-uniform query lengths during pooling prefill). Risk is an assertion at capture time. Mitigate by trying narrow `cudagraph_capture_sizes` first without changing `cudagraph_mode`.
- **dtype switch**: Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B may have been released and benchmarked at a specific dtype; changing it can shift cosine-sim outputs and silently break the contract that "measured throughput corresponds to released-model embeddings." Always pair with an output-equivalence guard.
- **TF32 matmul flag**: `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("high")` must be called before model parameters are loaded; setting it after has no effect on already-allocated tensors. Order-of-operations bug risk.
- **CPU pinning (P4)**: on shared cloud hosts, `sched_setaffinity` may be restricted by cgroup; needs to be best-effort.
- **K1 interaction**: with N=2 replicas, both must independently warm their CUDA graphs and FlashInfer cubins, doubling the startup time paid outside the timer - confirm the harness's warmup region budget allows this.
